statement.markdown: You are given an n x n integer matrix where each cell is filled with a distinct number from 1 to n^2. The matrix represents a grid of water cells where the value of each cell denotes the water level at that cell. You can move up, down, left, or right from one cell to another if the water level at the destination cell is not more than the water level at the source cell. You start at the top-left cell (0, 0) and want to reach the bottom-right cell (n - 1, n - 1). In each move, you can only move to an adjacent cell (up, down, left, or right) in the grid. Determine if it is possible to reach the bottom-right cell from the top-left cell following the given conditions.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer n, the size of the grid. Then, n lines follow, each containing n integers representing the water levels in the grid.
statement.outputFormat: A single boolean value (true or false) indicating whether it is possible to reach the bottom-right cell from the top-left cell.

constraints.description: The water levels in the grid.

examples.explanation: It is possible to move from the top-left cell to the bottom-right cell.
It is not possible to move from the top-left cell to the bottom-right cell.

hints.text: The problem involves navigating through a grid from the top-left cell to the bottom-right cell with certain constraints on movement based on water levels.
The movement constraints are such that you can move to an adjacent cell only if the water level at the destination cell is not more than the water level at the source cell.
This problem can be approached using a graph traversal algorithm, considering the grid as a graph where each cell is a node, and two nodes are connected if the cells are adjacent.

Since the water levels are distinct and the movement is restricted by water levels, using a priority queue or a similar data structure could be beneficial to keep track of the cells to visit next based on their water levels.

The core technique to solve this problem involves using a breadth-first search (BFS) or a similar traversal method, potentially combined with a data structure like a priority queue or a heap to efficiently explore the grid in ascending order of water levels.

editorial.title: Optimal Approach to Swim in Rising Water
editorial.intuition: The problem requires finding a path from the top-left to the bottom-right of a grid with rising water levels. A brute force approach would be too slow due to its high time complexity. The key observation is that using a priority queue can help efficiently explore the grid by always choosing the next cell with the lowest water level.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve trying all possible paths from the top-left to the bottom-right of the grid. This would result in an extremely high time complexity, likely exponential in the size of the grid, making it impractical for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: Using a priority queue to keep track of the next cell to visit based on the water level allows for a much more efficient exploration of the grid.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution uses a priority queue to efficiently explore the grid. It starts by adding the top-left cell to the queue, then iteratively removes the cell with the lowest water level and adds its unvisited neighbors back to the queue. The algorithm terminates when it reaches the bottom-right cell or exhausts all possible paths.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by showing that it always chooses the next cell with the lowest water level that can be reached. This ensures that the algorithm explores the grid in a way that minimizes the maximum water level encountered, which is necessary to find a valid path. The use of a priority queue guarantees that the algorithm runs in O(n^2 log n) time and O(n^2) space.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the algorithm with a sample input can help verify its correctness. For example, with a 2x2 grid [[0, 1], [2, 3]], the algorithm would correctly identify the path from top-left to bottom-right as feasible.
commonMistakes: Not using a priority queue to efficiently explore the grid
Not keeping track of visited cells to avoid revisiting them
Not considering the water level when choosing the next cell to visit
Not terminating the algorithm when the bottom-right cell is reached
Not handling edge cases such as a grid with a single cell
Not verifying the correctness of the algorithm with sample inputs
